programs.name: Aged and Disabled (AD) HCBS Waiver
programs.tagline: Home care services for parents who qualify for Medicaid and need nursing home-level care but want to stay home.

localEntities.name: DHHS (Department of Health and Human Services) - primary administrator
Blue Rivers AAA - regional Area Agency on Aging
AmanaCare - listed as service provider/application assistance

programs.intro:
If your loved one needs help with bathing, dressing, or medication management but wants to avoid nursing home placement, Nebraska's Aged and Disabled HCBS Waiver provides home care services at no cost. This Medicaid waiver covers personal care assistance, light housekeeping, meal preparation, medical transportation, and emergency response systems for qualifying residents.

Your loved one must be 65+ or have a qualifying disability, already be enrolled in Nebraska Medicaid, and meet Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements (needing help with at least 3 daily activities like toileting, bathing, or eating). Because this waiver is designed as an alternative to nursing home care, your loved one's home equity cannot exceed $752,000 in 2026.

This is a two-step process: first qualify for Nebraska Medicaid, then apply for the waiver. Level of Care assessment is scheduled within 14 days of waiver application submission.

summary: Age 65+ OR any age with qualifying disability
Must already be enrolled in Nebraska Medicaid
Need nursing facility level of care

exemptAssets: Primary home if spouse, minor child (17 or younger), or permanently disabled child lives there

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must demonstrate limited functioning with at least 3 Activities of Daily Living (toileting, bathing, dressing, transferring, eating) plus at least 1 risk factor or medical condition, OR limited functioning with at least 1 ADL plus at least 1 risk factor AND at least 1 cognitive limitation.
otherRequirements: U.S. citizen or qualified alien lawfully present
Nebraska resident
Enrolled in Nebraska Medicaid
Need for at least one waiver service to maintain community living

steps.description: DHHS will schedule your functional assessment within 14 days. This clinical evaluation determines if you meet nursing facility level of care requirements.

steps.title: Safety assessment and service planning
steps.description: If you qualify for Level of Care, a safety assessment determines if waiver services can safely meet your needs at home. Service plan is developed based on your specific care requirements.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Level of Care assessment scheduled within 14 days of application submission
applicationGuide.tip: Gather all medical records showing your loved one's daily care needs before applying — the Level of Care assessment is the key requirement that determines eligibility.

applicationNotes: You cannot apply for the AD Waiver until you are already enrolled in Nebraska Medicaid — this is a strict prerequisite, not something that happens automatically
The Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment uses specific clinical criteria — having a disability or being elderly alone does not guarantee qualification
Home equity limit of $752,000 is for 2026 and may change annually — verify current limits when applying
The waiver program expires 07/31/2026 and requires federal renewal — confirm current program status before applying

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply for this waiver if they're not on Medicaid yet?
faqs.answer: No. Your loved one must be enrolled in Nebraska Medicaid before applying for the AD Waiver. Apply for Medicaid first through DHHS at 877-667-6266, then submit the waiver application once Medicaid enrollment is confirmed.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's house is worth more than $752,000?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify if a spouse, minor child (17 or younger), or permanently disabled child of any age lives in the home. Otherwise, the home equity cannot exceed $752,000 in 2026. Home equity is calculated as current home value minus outstanding mortgage balance.

faqs.question: How do I know if my loved one meets Nursing Facility Level of Care?
faqs.answer: Your loved one needs limited functioning with at least 3 Activities of Daily Living (like bathing, dressing, toileting) plus a medical condition, OR limited functioning with at least 1 ADL plus both a risk factor and cognitive limitation. DHHS conducts this clinical assessment — you cannot self-determine eligibility.

faqs.question: What happens if the safety assessment shows home care isn't safe?
faqs.answer: If the assessment determines that waiver services cannot safely meet your loved one's care needs at home, they will not qualify for the waiver even if they meet Level of Care requirements. The program is designed only for people who can be safely cared for in the community.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can submit applications on behalf of elderly loved ones. You'll need to provide all required documentation and may need legal authorization if your loved one cannot participate in the assessment process due to cognitive impairment.

faqs.question: How many hours of care will my loved one receive?
faqs.answer: Service hours are determined individually based on your loved one's assessed needs and safety requirements. The program covers personal care, housekeeping, meal preparation, and medical transportation, but specific hours and frequency depend on the individualized service plan developed after approval.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: All medical care, transportation, and daily support coordinated under one program for parents who need nursing home-level care but want to stay home.

localEntities.name: Immanuel Pathways Eastern Nebraska (Omaha; serves Douglas, Sarpy, parts of Cass, Dodge, Saunders, Washington Counties)

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 55+ and needs significant daily help but wants to avoid a nursing home, PACE provides all their medical care, medications, transportation, and support services through one coordinated team. Because PACE covers everything from doctor visits to adult day care to home health under one program, your loved one won't need separate Medicare providers — the PACE team manages all care coordination.

Your loved one must qualify for nursing home-level care (needing daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management) but be able to live safely at home with PACE services. In Nebraska, PACE is only available through Immanuel Pathways in the Omaha metro area. If your loved one has Medicare and Medicaid, there are no co-pays or deductibles. Private pay is available but typically costs $7,000+ per month.

summary: Able to live safely in community with services
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment of whether your loved one needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other activities of daily living)
otherRequirements: Live in Douglas, Sarpy, or select zip codes in Cass, Dodge, Saunders, or Washington Counties
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage
Must be able to live safely in community with PACE services

steps.title: Enrollment and care planning
steps.description: Once approved, interdisciplinary team creates personalized care plan covering all medical, social, and support services.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Potential wait times depending on capacity — ask about current availability when calling
applicationGuide.tip: If your loved one is enrolled in Medicare Advantage, they'll need to disenroll first — PACE cannot work alongside Medicare Advantage plans.

applicationNotes: You cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage (Part C) and PACE at the same time — disenrollment from Medicare Advantage required first
PACE becomes your loved one's sole source of Medicare and Medicaid services — they cannot use other providers outside the PACE network
Private pay costs typically exceed $7,000 per month if your loved one doesn't qualify for Medicaid coverage
Your loved one can disenroll from PACE at any time and return to regular Medicare/Medicaid, but re-enrollment isn't guaranteed

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house and still qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is exempt from PACE asset limits. However, for Medicaid coverage within PACE, total countable assets must be $2,000 or less. Your loved one can participate in PACE as private pay without asset limits, though this typically costs $7,000+ per month.

faqs.question: What if my loved one lives outside the Omaha metro area?
faqs.answer: PACE is only available in Douglas and Sarpy Counties, plus select zip codes in Cass, Dodge, Saunders, and Washington Counties. If your loved one lives outside this service area, they cannot participate in Nebraska's PACE program. Contact Immanuel Pathways to verify if their specific address is covered.

faqs.question: Can my loved one still see their current doctor?
faqs.answer: No, PACE provides all medical care through its own interdisciplinary team. Your loved one must receive all services through PACE providers — they cannot continue seeing outside doctors or specialists. The PACE team coordinates all care, which eliminates the need for separate providers but requires giving up existing doctor relationships.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's condition worsens and they need a nursing home?
faqs.answer: PACE covers nursing home care when needed, and the same interdisciplinary team continues managing care even in a facility. Because PACE is designed to serve people who need nursing home-level care, the program can adapt as your loved one's needs change without requiring new applications or assessments.

faqs.question: How long does the eligibility assessment really take?
faqs.answer: The assessment includes medical, functional, and financial reviews that can take several weeks to complete. The PACE team must certify your loved one meets nursing facility level of care requirements through comprehensive evaluation. Contact Immanuel Pathways directly for current processing timelines.

faqs.question: Can I apply for PACE while my loved one is in the hospital?
faqs.answer: Yes, hospital discharge planners can initiate PACE enrollment, and this often speeds the process. PACE can coordinate discharge planning and ensure services are in place when your loved one returns home. Contact the PACE program as soon as possible during hospitalization to begin assessment.

faqs.question: What if my loved one has Medicare Advantage?
faqs.answer: Your loved one must disenroll from Medicare Advantage before joining PACE — the two programs cannot work together. You can disenroll from Medicare Advantage during open enrollment (October 15 - December 7) or within 12 months of first joining a Medicare Advantage plan. Call Medicare at (800) 633-4227 for disenrollment assistance.

faqs.question: Can my loved one leave PACE if they don't like it?
faqs.answer: Yes, your loved one can disenroll from PACE at any time and return to regular Medicare and Medicaid. However, re-enrollment in PACE isn't guaranteed if they want to return later. During the transition, help your loved one establish new relationships with Medicare providers before disenrolling.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Program (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.tagline: If your loved one qualifies, Nebraska will pay their Medicare premiums and possibly all Medicare out-of-pocket costs.

localEntities.name: Local DHHS Service and Support Centers statewide; primary via ACCESSNebraska.

programs.intro:
If your loved one is on Medicare and has limited income, Nebraska's Medicare Savings Program can eliminate their Medicare costs entirely. The program has three tiers: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary) pays all Medicare premiums, deductibles, and co-pays for those with incomes up to $1,350/month ($1,824 for couples). SLMB and QI programs pay Medicare Part B premiums only for slightly higher incomes up to $1,823/month for individuals.

Because this program also automatically qualifies your loved one for Extra Help with prescription drug costs, the combined savings can reach $3,000-5,000 per year. Your loved one can have higher assets than regular Medicaid allows — up to $9,950 for individuals ($14,910 for couples) — making this accessible to more families.

Nebraska administers this program statewide with no waiting list for QMB and SLMB, though QI funding may be limited. You apply through the same system used for Medicaid, and approval typically takes 45 days.

contentSections.title: Which Program Tier Fits Your Parent?
contentSections.content:
**QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)** - Income up to $1,350/month individual, $1,824/month couple. Pays ALL Medicare costs: premiums, deductibles, co-pays.

**SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary)** - Income $1,350-$1,620/month individual. Pays Medicare Part B premiums only.

**QI (Qualifying Individual)** - Income $1,620-$1,823/month individual. Pays Medicare Part B premiums only. Limited funding available.

contentSections.title: Asset Limits Are Higher Than Regular Medicaid
contentSections.content: Medicare Savings Programs allow $9,950 in countable assets for individuals ($14,910 for couples) compared to just $4,000/$6,000 for regular Nebraska Medicaid. Your loved one's home, one car, and burial plots don't count toward these limits.

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply for Medicare Savings Program if they were denied regular Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes. Medicare Savings Programs have higher asset limits ($9,950 vs $4,000 for individuals) and may be available even when regular Medicaid isn't. The income limits are also calculated differently.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's income is right at the border between QMB and SLMB?
faqs.answer: If your loved one qualifies for QMB (income up to $1,350/month), they get much more coverage — all Medicare costs paid vs. just premiums. Even $50/month over the QMB limit means thousands less in annual savings.

faqs.question: Does my loved one's house count toward the $9,950 asset limit?
faqs.answer: No. Their primary residence is exempt regardless of value, along with one vehicle, burial plots, and life insurance up to $1,500 face value. Only bank accounts, investments, and additional property count toward the limit.

faqs.question: How long does it take to get approved and when do benefits start?
faqs.answer: Nebraska typically processes applications within 45 days. If approved, QMB benefits are retroactive to the month you applied, potentially reimbursing Medicare costs you already paid that month.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you have legal authority (power of attorney, guardianship, or representative payee status). You can also help them apply, but they may need to sign documents unless you have specific legal authorization.

faqs.question: What happens at the annual review?
faqs.answer: Nebraska will mail renewal forms asking for updated income and asset information. You must respond by the deadline or benefits will end. The review process is similar to the initial application but usually faster.

faqs.question: Can my loved one have Medicare Savings Program and Extra Help at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes — in fact, Medicare Savings Program automatically qualifies your loved one for Extra Help with prescription drug costs. This combination can save $3,000-5,000 per year depending on their medication needs.

faqs.question: What if my loved one gets QI program but then loses it due to funding limits?
faqs.answer: QI funding is limited and allocated annually. If funding runs out, your loved one keeps benefits through the end of that calendar year but may need to reapply early the following year. QMB and SLMB don't have these funding restrictions.

programs.name: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)
programs.tagline: Monthly grocery assistance for seniors on fixed incomes — up to $546/month for a couple

localEntities.name: Local DHHS Service and Support Centers statewide

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 60+ and struggling with grocery costs, SNAP provides monthly food benefits loaded onto an EBT card. Because elderly households get special treatment under SNAP rules, your loved one may qualify even if their income seems too high for other programs — there's no gross income limit for seniors unless they're well above poverty level.

Benefit amounts depend on household size and net income after deductions. A single person can receive up to $546/month, while a couple can get up to $546/month maximum. Because medical expenses over $35/month and high utility costs reduce your loved one's countable income, many seniors qualify for more than they expect.

The program covers groceries at most stores but not restaurant meals or hot prepared foods. Benefits last 12 months before recertification, and Nebraska processes applications within 30 days (7 days if income is very low).
programs.savingsSource: Monthly SNAP benefits range from $100-546 based on household size and income

exemptAssets: life insurance cash value (if not income-producing)

faqs.question: Can I apply for SNAP on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, but your loved one must sign the application or designate you as their authorized representative. Call 1-800-383-4278 to discuss the process if your loved one cannot sign due to physical or mental limitations.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's income is slightly over the limit?
faqs.answer: Medical expenses over $35/month and utility costs can reduce countable income significantly. A senior paying $200/month in medical expenses might qualify even with income that appears too high. The utility deduction alone can be up to $615/month for heating and cooling.

faqs.question: Does my loved one's house count against the $4,500 asset limit?
faqs.answer: No, the primary residence is completely exempt regardless of value. Also exempt: household goods, retirement accounts, one vehicle per adult, and life insurance that doesn't produce income.

faqs.question: Can my loved one use SNAP benefits for meal delivery or hot food?
faqs.answer: No, SNAP only covers groceries you prepare at home. However, some elderly or disabled individuals may qualify for Restaurant Meals Program in certain areas — ask your caseworker about availability.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's Social Security increases during the year?
faqs.answer: Report income changes within 10 days. Because elderly households have 12-month certification periods, small increases usually won't affect benefits until the next renewal unless the change is substantial.

faqs.question: How long do SNAP benefits last if my loved one is approved?
faqs.answer: Benefits continue for 12 months, then your loved one must recertify. Nebraska will send renewal forms about 60 days before expiration. Elderly and disabled households get the longest certification periods available.

programs.name: Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.tagline: Get help paying heating and cooling bills when your loved one's income is tight

localEntities.name: DHHS Public Assistance Offices statewide

programs.intro:
If your loved one struggles to pay heating or cooling bills on a fixed income, LIHEAP provides a one-time payment directly to the utility company each year. Your loved one may qualify if their household income is below $23,475 for one person or $31,725 for two people (2026 limits).

The program runs October through March for heating assistance and during summer months for cooling help. Because funding is limited, applications can close early once money runs out — so apply as soon as the season opens. Crisis assistance is available year-round for emergencies like shutoff notices or broken heating equipment.

Parents age 60 and older get priority processing, which can speed up approval. If your loved one already receives other Nebraska assistance like Economic Assistance, they're automatically eligible and don't need to apply separately.
programs.savingsSource: One-time utility payment amount varies by household circumstances

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply for both heating and cooling assistance in the same year?
faqs.answer: Yes, these are separate benefits. Heating assistance runs October 1 through March 31, and cooling assistance runs during summer months. Your loved one can apply for both if they meet income requirements during each season.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's utility company isn't cooperating with LIHEAP payments?
faqs.answer: LIHEAP payments go directly to utility companies, and they're required to accept them. If you encounter problems, contact your local DHHS office at dhhs.ne.gov/Pages/Public-Assistance-Offices.aspx — they can work with the utility company to resolve issues.

faqs.question: Does my loved one's home value count against them for LIHEAP eligibility?
faqs.answer: The program considers resources and assets in benefit calculation, but the sources don't specify if home value counts or what the limits are. Call 1-800-383-4278 to ask about your loved one's specific situation.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one who has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply for elderly loved ones who cannot manage their affairs. You may need to provide documentation of your authority to act on their behalf, such as power of attorney papers.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one gets approved but then their income changes?
faqs.answer: LIHEAP is a one-time annual benefit, so changes after approval won't affect the current year's payment. However, income changes will be considered when they reapply for the following year's assistance.

faqs.question: How much will LIHEAP actually pay toward my loved one's utility bill?
faqs.answer: The payment amount varies based on household income, assets, size, fuel type, and housing type using a formula that disregards 20% of earned income. The exact amount isn't specified in program materials — you'll find out the payment amount when approved.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get LIHEAP and Weatherization assistance at the same time?
faqs.answer: These are separate programs with different eligibility requirements. Your loved one may qualify for both, but weatherization has its own application process. Ask about weatherization when you apply for LIHEAP since they're related energy programs.

faqs.question: What counts as a crisis situation for emergency LIHEAP help?
faqs.answer: Crisis assistance is for emergencies like utility shutoff notices, broken heating or cooling equipment, or dangerous heating situations. Crisis help is available year-round and may get processed within 48 hours compared to the normal 30-day timeline.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.tagline: Free home energy improvements like insulation and furnace repair if your loved one's income is below $3,825/month for two people

localEntities.name: United Way of the Midlands (Douglas County/Omaha), Northeast Nebraska Community Action Partnership (14 counties: Antelope, Burt, Cedar, Cuming, Dakota, Dixon, Dodge, Knox, Madison, Pierce, Stanton, Thurston, Washington, Wayne), Community Action Partnership of Mid-Nebraska (27 south central counties), Nebraska Dept of Environment and Energy (state admin)

programs.intro:
If your loved one struggles with high heating bills or drafty rooms, Nebraska's Weatherization Assistance Program provides free home energy improvements worth thousands of dollars. The program installs insulation, replaces furnaces, seals air leaks, and conducts safety testing at no cost to households earning below 200% of the federal poverty level — that's $3,825/month for a two-person household in 2026.

Because the program prioritizes households with adults age 60 and older, your loved one may move up the waitlist faster than younger applicants. Parents who already receive SSI, TANF, or LIHEAP automatically qualify regardless of income — no additional income verification needed.

Nebraska serves different regions through local providers, so where your loved one lives determines which agency handles their application. Douglas County residents apply through United Way of the Midlands, while 14 northeast counties work with NENCAP, and 27 south-central counties go through Community Action Partnership of Mid-Nebraska.

summary: Income at or below 200% of federal poverty level
Automatic qualification if receiving SSI, TANF, or LIHEAP
otherRequirements: Home must have weatherization needs that meet program criteria
Renters need written landlord permission
Property must be owner-occupied or renter-occupied single-family, multi-family, mobile home, or frame home

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply if they rent their home?
faqs.answer: Yes, renters are eligible, but your loved one must get written permission from their landlord before any weatherization work can begin. The landlord cannot charge rent increases for 3 years after improvements in some cases. This applies to single-family homes, apartments, and mobile homes.

faqs.question: What if my loved one already receives LIHEAP energy assistance?
faqs.answer: Parents receiving LIHEAP automatically qualify for weatherization regardless of income limits. They just need to show their LIHEAP approval from the current year or previous 12 months. The same applies to SSI and TANF recipients.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really?
faqs.answer: Wait times vary significantly by region. Northeast Nebraska serves about 100 homes per year across 14 counties, creating substantial waits. Douglas County through United Way may have different capacity. Households with seniors age 60+ get priority, potentially shortening waits.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's income goes up while waiting?
faqs.answer: If weatherization work hasn't started within 12 months, your loved one must re-verify their income and household size. If income now exceeds 200% of poverty level and they don't receive SSI/TANF/LIHEAP, they'll lose eligibility.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, adult children can help with applications, but the eligible household member (your loved one) typically needs to sign forms and provide their income information. Contact your regional provider to clarify their specific requirements for representative applications.

faqs.question: Will they replace my loved one's old furnace?
faqs.answer: Furnace repair or replacement is available, but only for homeowners (not renters). The program evaluates whether repair or replacement provides better energy savings. All furnace work includes combustion safety testing and carbon monoxide checks.

faqs.question: What if we live in a county not mentioned in the main providers?
faqs.answer: Nebraska divides all counties among regional providers. If you're unsure which provider serves your area, call 2-1-1 or visit dee.nebraska.gov for referral to the correct regional agency. Every county has a designated weatherization provider.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get weatherization and LIHEAP in the same year?
faqs.answer: Yes, these programs complement each other. LIHEAP helps pay current energy bills while weatherization makes long-term improvements to reduce future bills. In fact, LIHEAP recipients automatically qualify for weatherization services.

programs.name: Nebraska SHIP (State Health Insurance Assistance Program)
programs.tagline: Free one-on-one Medicare counseling to help your loved one navigate plans, costs, and coverage options.

localEntities.name: Nebraska Department of Insurance (PO Box 95087, Lincoln NE 68509-5087)
Local: Northeast Nebraska AAA, Volunteers Assisting Seniors (Omaha), West Central Nebraska AAA (North Platte), Kearney office

programs.intro:
If your loved one is eligible for Medicare, Nebraska SHIP provides free, unbiased counseling to help them understand their options. Certified counselors explain Medicare eligibility, benefits, Medicare Supplement Insurance, Prescription Drug Plans, Medicare Advantage Plans, and programs that help pay Medicare costs for those with limited income.

Because SHIP counselors don't sell insurance or recommend specific companies, your loved one gets objective information to make informed decisions. The service is available to all Medicare-eligible individuals, family members, and caregivers with no income or asset limits.

SHIP also helps with applying for Extra Help (prescription drug cost assistance) and Medicare Savings Programs, and provides Medicare fraud prevention education. You can reach counselors by phone, email, or through local offices statewide.

otherRequirements: Must be eligible for Medicare or a family member/caregiver of a Medicare beneficiary

applicationGuide.summary: Call the toll-free number to arrange free counseling — no formal application needed.

steps.title: Call Nebraska SHIP

steps.title: Schedule counseling session
steps.description: Work with staff to schedule one-on-one counseling by phone or in-person through their statewide network of certified counselors.

steps.title: Get personalized guidance
steps.description: Receive unbiased information about Medicare options, assistance with applications for cost-saving programs, and answers to specific questions about your loved one's situation.

applicationGuide.processingTime: No formal application or processing; counseling arranged directly upon contact
applicationGuide.tip: Have your loved one's Medicare card and current insurance information ready when you call to make the session more productive.

contacts.description: Free Medicare counseling and assistance

faqs.question: Can I call on behalf of my loved one who has Medicare?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members and caregivers can contact Nebraska SHIP for information and guidance about Medicare options for their loved one. Counselors provide services to Medicare beneficiaries, their families, and caregivers with no restrictions.

faqs.question: Will counselors recommend specific insurance companies or policies?
faqs.answer: No, SHIP counselors provide unbiased information only — they don't sell insurance, recommend specific companies, or endorse particular policies. This ensures you get objective guidance to help your loved one make informed decisions about their Medicare coverage.

faqs.question: Can SHIP help if my loved one needs financial assistance with Medicare costs?
faqs.answer: Yes, counselors can explain and help apply for programs like Extra Help (prescription drug cost assistance) and Medicare Savings Programs for people with limited income and resources. They'll assess eligibility and guide you through the application process.

faqs.question: Is there a difference between calling the state number versus local offices?
faqs.answer: No, all offices provide the same services, eligibility criteria, and wait times. You can call the statewide number at 1-800-234-7119 or contact local partners like Area Agencies on Aging for the same free, certified counseling.

programs.name: Home-Delivered Meals (via Area Agencies on Aging)
programs.tagline: If your loved one can't leave home for meals, they may qualify for free home-delivered meals Monday through Friday.

localEntities.name: Area Agencies on Aging

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 60+ and homebound due to illness, disability, or isolation, they may qualify for free nutritionally balanced meals delivered to their home Monday through Friday. Each meal meets one-third of daily recommended dietary needs for older adults and is approved by a registered dietitian. Some locations also offer weekend meals for extreme needs.

The program prioritizes low-income older adults, but there are no specific income or asset limits — eligibility focuses on being homebound and unable to attend group meals at senior centers. Because each Area Agency on Aging defines "homebound" differently, you'll need to contact your local agency to confirm your loved one's eligibility.

Meals are completely free, though agencies request voluntary contributions (these are suggestions, not bills or required fees). Spouses of eligible individuals and dependent adults with disabilities living in the home may also receive meals if it helps maintain the older adult at home.

summary: Homebound due to illness, disability, or isolation
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be homebound by reason of illness, incapacitating disability, or otherwise isolated (definition varies by local Area Agency on Aging)
otherRequirements: Spouse of eligible person (60+) can receive meals regardless of age
Dependent individual with disability living with eligible person can receive meals

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging by phone for eligibility determination — meals can start immediately with written assessment within 2 weeks.

steps.title: Begin meal delivery
steps.description: If eligible, meals can begin immediately. Volunteers or drivers deliver meals Monday through Friday, with some locations offering weekend delivery for extreme needs.

steps.title: Complete written assessment
steps.description: Within 2 weeks of the first meal, the agency will complete a written assessment including nutrition-related supportive services and referrals.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Meals can begin immediately after phone screening, with written assessment completed within 2 weeks of first meal

applicationGuide.tip: Ask specifically about weekend meal availability if your loved one has extreme needs — not all locations offer this service.

Priority is given to those with greatest economic and social need, so mention any financial hardships or social isolation
Voluntary contribution requests are not bills — you cannot be denied meals for inability to pay

faqs.question: What if my loved one can occasionally leave home but has trouble getting to meal sites?
faqs.answer: Each Area Agency on Aging defines 'homebound' differently. Some focus strictly on medical inability to leave home, while others consider transportation barriers or social isolation. Call your local agency to discuss your loved one's specific situation — they make case-by-case determinations.

faqs.question: Can I apply for my loved one if they have dementia or can't make phone calls?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can contact the Area Agency on Aging on behalf of their loved one. The agency will work with you to assess eligibility and arrange meal delivery. Be prepared to describe your loved one's condition and why they cannot attend group meals.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really?
faqs.answer: Waitlists vary significantly by region and are only established when providers cannot serve all eligible individuals. Those with greatest economic need, limited English proficiency, and rural residents get priority. Contact your local Area Agency on Aging for current wait times in your specific area.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's condition improves and they can start leaving home?
faqs.answer: Agencies conduct annual reassessments or review eligibility when circumstances change. If your loved one can attend congregate meals at senior centers, they may transition to that program. The goal is connecting people to the most appropriate meal service for their current needs.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get both home-delivered meals and SNAP food stamps?
faqs.answer: Yes, these are separate programs. Home-delivered meals provide one meal per day Monday-Friday, while SNAP helps with grocery purchases. Many people receive both to ensure adequate nutrition throughout the week and on weekends.

faqs.question: What if we can't afford the suggested contribution?
faqs.answer: Voluntary contributions are never required — they're suggestions, not bills. Your loved one cannot be denied meals or have service discontinued for inability to pay. The program specifically prohibits making contribution statements look like required fees.

programs.name: Lifespan Respite Services
programs.tagline: Up to $125 monthly to hire respite care when you're caring for a family member with disabilities or chronic conditions.

localEntities.name: Nebraska Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS) - statewide Nebraska Lifespan Respite Network

programs.intro:
If you're providing full-time care for a loved one with disabilities, chronic illness, or conditions requiring constant supervision, Nebraska's Lifespan Respite Services provides up to $125 monthly to hire temporary care. This covers in-home care, adult day programs, or short-term facility stays so you can take breaks from caregiving.

The program serves families with limited income and assets who are caring for someone with developmental disabilities, brain injury, mental illness, visual or hearing impairments, or other conditions requiring ongoing supervision. Because this targets families already stretched financially, income and asset verification may be required to demonstrate financial need.

Crisis situations (like caregiver illness or emergency) may qualify for additional funding up to $2,000, but this requires a separate application and approval process beyond the monthly benefit.
programs.savingsSource: Up to $125 monthly for respite services

faqs.question: Can my loved one get this help if they already receive services through Medicaid?
faqs.answer: It depends on the specific services. You cannot receive Lifespan Respite if your loved one is already getting respite services or subsidies from another government program. However, if Medicaid covers other services but not respite care, you may still be eligible.

faqs.question: How much money can my family have in savings and still qualify?
faqs.answer: The program considers liquid assets including cash, checking and savings accounts, CDs, stocks, bonds, life insurance cash values, and retirement funds. Exact dollar limits aren't specified, but you must demonstrate financial need. The state may request verification of all assets.

faqs.question: What counts as 'special needs' that would make my loved one eligible?
faqs.answer: Your loved one must have medical, mental, behavioral, or disability conditions requiring full-time ongoing supervision or care. This includes developmental disabilities, brain injury, mental illness, hearing or visual impairments, orthopedic conditions, or other health impairments requiring constant caregiving.

faqs.question: Can I use the $125 monthly benefit for overnight care at a facility?
faqs.answer: Yes, the benefit covers respite services in three settings: in-home care, community programs, or facility-based care. You can use it for whatever type of temporary care works best for your situation and your loved one's needs.

faqs.question: What if I need emergency respite because I get sick or have a family crisis?
faqs.answer: Crisis situations may qualify for additional funding up to $2,000 through Exceptional Circumstances funding. However, this requires a completely separate application and approval process beyond the regular monthly benefit program.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really, and who gets priority?
faqs.answer: Wait times aren't specified, but the program establishes priorities and waiting lists based on the care recipient's special needs. Families with higher risk of the care recipient needing out-of-home placement may receive higher priority.

faqs.question: Can a paid caregiver apply, or does it have to be family?
faqs.answer: You must be an unpaid primary caregiver who is a loved one, spouse, family member, or friend providing continuous full-time care. Paid professional caregivers are not eligible to apply for this program.

faqs.question: What happens if my income changes after I'm approved?
faqs.answer: Since the program has ongoing financial eligibility requirements, you'll likely need to report income changes. The state may request updated asset and income verification at any time to ensure you continue meeting financial need criteria.

programs.name: Nebraska Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.tagline: Part-time community service work at minimum wage that builds skills and leads to permanent employment for adults 55+

localEntities.name: National Able (primary)
Eastern Nebraska Office on Aging
Aging Partners
Experience Works

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 55+ and unemployed with limited income, they may qualify for part-time community service work that pays minimum wage while building job skills. SCSEP provides typically 20 hours per week at non-profits, with training in areas like childcare, customer service, computer skills, or healthcare that leads to permanent employment.

Participants earn minimum wage for their community service hours while receiving job training and placement assistance. Because the program focuses on skill development, your loved one will work with an Individual Employment Plan (IEP) tailored to their career goals and attend regular training sessions.

Availability depends on funding in your area — the program operates through specific organizations like National Able, Eastern Nebraska Office on Aging, and Aging Partners. Veterans, adults 65+, and rural residents get priority for available positions.

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply if they have a small pension or Social Security income?
faqs.answer: Yes, if their total household income is below 125% of the federal poverty level. Social Security, pensions, SSDI, and SSI all count toward the income limit, but the exact dollar threshold depends on household size and current federal poverty guidelines.

faqs.question: What happens if there's no funding available in our area?
faqs.answer: Your loved one may be placed on a waiting list until funding becomes available. The program operates through specific organizations like National Able and its subgrantees, so availability varies by geographic area and annual funding levels.

faqs.question: Can my loved one choose what type of community service work they do?
faqs.answer: Work assignments depend on available positions and your loved one's Individual Employment Plan (IEP). Common placements include childcare, customer service, teachers' aide, computer technician, building maintenance, and healthcare support roles.

faqs.question: How long can my loved one stay in the SCSEP program?
faqs.answer: The program focuses on transitioning participants to permanent employment. Length of participation varies, but the goal is developing skills for unsubsidized employment. Semi-annual reassessments track progress toward employment goals.

faqs.question: Will working in SCSEP affect my loved one's Social Security or other benefits?
faqs.answer: SCSEP wages may affect SSI and other income-based benefits. Contact Social Security Administration at (800) 772-1213 to understand how part-time employment income will impact their specific benefits.

faqs.question: What if my loved one has a disability — can they still participate?
faqs.answer: Yes, people with disabilities actually get priority placement in SCSEP. The program can accommodate various disabilities, and disability benefits count toward income eligibility rather than disqualifying participation.

programs.name: Legal Services for Seniors (via AAAs)
programs.tagline: Free legal help for Nebraska seniors 60+ with wills, power of attorney, Medicaid questions, and consumer protection issues.

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 60+ and needs legal help they can't afford, Nebraska's Area Agencies on Aging provide free legal advice and brief services. This covers essential issues like simple wills, power of attorney documents, Medicaid applications, consumer protection, tenant problems, and bankruptcy guidance.

The program serves seniors with economic or social needs through eight regional offices statewide. You can get immediate phone advice from experienced attorneys through the ElderAccessLine (1-800-527-7249), and some regions offer expanded services including negotiation and limited representation. Because this focuses on "brief services," it's designed for advice and document preparation — not full litigation support.

otherRequirements: Nebraska residency

faqs.question: What if I call outside the ElderAccessLine hours?
faqs.answer: The ElderAccessLine only operates Monday–Thursday 9am–noon and 1–3pm, plus Friday 9am–noon Central Time. Calls outside these hours cannot be answered. You can also contact your regional Area Agency on Aging directly during regular business hours or visit legalaidofnebraska.org for general information.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get full legal representation through this program?
faqs.answer: This program provides "brief services" — advice, counseling, and limited representation for specific issues. It's not designed for full litigation or complex legal proceedings. Some regions like Lancaster County have contracted attorneys who can provide negotiation services, but availability varies by area.

faqs.question: How do I know if my loved one meets the income and asset limits?
faqs.answer: Income and asset limits aren't published statewide — only Lancaster County documents specific limits ($12,000 individual, $24,000 couple in assets). You must contact your regional Area Agency on Aging to learn the eligibility criteria for your area. The program serves seniors with "economic or social needs," but each region defines this differently.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.tagline: Free advocacy when your loved one has concerns about their nursing home or assisted living facility care.

localEntities.name: State office: PO Box 95026, Lincoln, NE 68509-5026

programs.intro:
If your loved one lives in a nursing home, assisted living facility, or other long-term care setting and has concerns about their care, treatment, or rights, Nebraska's Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program provides free advocacy services. The program investigates complaints, educates families about residents' rights, and works to resolve problems between residents and facilities.

Because ombudsmen are independent advocates (not employed by facilities or the state), they can objectively investigate concerns about health, safety, welfare, or rights violations. Services are completely confidential unless your loved one explicitly gives permission to share information. Anyone can contact the program — residents, family members, friends, or facility staff — and there are no age, income, or other eligibility restrictions.

summary: Family members can call on behalf of residents
otherRequirements: Must involve a resident of nursing homes, assisted living facilities, board and care homes, hospice centers, adult day care, or long-term rehabilitation centers

applicationGuide.summary: Call the statewide hotline at (800) 942-7830 — no forms or documentation required.

steps.title: Call the statewide ombudsman hotline
steps.description: Dial (800) 942-7830 to speak with an ombudsman about your loved one's situation. You can also visit https://dhhs.ne.gov/Pages/Aging-Ombudsman.aspx for more information.

steps.title: Explain the concern
steps.description: Describe the issue your loved one is experiencing — whether it's about care quality, staff treatment, facility policies, or rights violations. The ombudsman will determine next steps.

steps.title: Investigation begins
steps.description: The ombudsman will investigate the complaint confidentially and work with the facility to resolve the issue. They may visit the facility, interview staff, and review policies.

applicationGuide.tip: You don't need your loved one's permission to call and ask questions about their rights, but the ombudsman will need permission before they can investigate a specific complaint.

faqs.question: Can I call the ombudsman if my loved one doesn't want to make waves at their facility?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can call to ask general questions about residents' rights and get advice on how to handle situations. However, for the ombudsman to investigate a specific complaint or visit the facility, they typically need permission from your loved one. Services are confidential, but facilities will know an investigation is happening.

faqs.question: What if the facility retaliates against my loved one for filing a complaint?
faqs.answer: Retaliation against residents for filing complaints is illegal under federal and state law. If you suspect retaliation, immediately report it to the ombudsman at (800) 942-7830. The program specifically monitors for and investigates retaliation cases.

faqs.question: How long does it take for the ombudsman to investigate a complaint?
faqs.answer: Investigation timelines vary depending on the complexity and severity of the complaint. The program prioritizes cases involving immediate health and safety concerns. Contact (800) 942-7830 for specific expectations about your situation.

programs.name: Aged and Disabled Waiver
programs.tagline: Nebraska Medicaid waiver that keeps your loved one at home instead of a nursing facility by covering personal care, home modifications, and assisted living.

localEntities.name: DHHS central office in Lincoln; regional like AOWN in western Nebraska

programs.intro:
If your loved one needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management but wants to avoid a nursing home, Nebraska's Aged and Disabled Waiver may cover the care they need at home or in assisted living. This Medicaid waiver pays for personal care services, home modifications, adult day health programs, and assisted living — services that regular Medicaid doesn't cover.

Your loved one must be 65+ or disabled at any age, qualify for Nebraska Medicaid, and meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment showing they need significant daily assistance with at least 3 activities like toileting, bathing, or mobility). Because this waiver allows people to receive care outside nursing facilities, it can help your loved one stay in familiar surroundings while getting professional support.

Home equity cannot exceed $752,000 in 2026, though the family home is typically exempt if your loved one lives there or intends to return. The program covers a wide range of services through individualized care plans, but all services combined cannot cost more than what Medicaid would pay for nursing home care.

summary: Age 65+ or disabled at any age
Need nursing facility level of care

exemptAssets: Primary residence if living there or intend to return

otherRequirements: Nebraska resident and U.S. citizen or qualified alien
Maintain Nebraska Medicaid eligibility
Participate in Person-Centered Care Plan
Cost of services cannot exceed nursing facility rates

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house if it's worth more than $752,000?
faqs.answer: No, if your loved one's home equity exceeds $752,000 in 2026, they won't qualify for the waiver. However, the home is completely exempt if your spouse, minor child, or permanently disabled child lives there. The equity limit only applies to the person receiving waiver services.

faqs.question: What if my loved one doesn't qualify for regular Medicaid due to income?
faqs.answer: Your loved one might still qualify through Medicaid's special income rules for long-term care services. Call (877) 667-6266 to discuss your loved one's specific financial situation — there are different income limits for people who need nursing facility level of care.

faqs.question: How long does the functional assessment take and what should we expect?
faqs.answer: The interRAI-HC assessment is conducted in your loved one's home and evaluates their ability to perform daily activities like bathing, dressing, toileting, and medication management. The assessor also looks at cognitive function, safety risks, and medical conditions. This determines whether your loved one meets nursing facility level of care.

faqs.question: Can my loved one receive both AD Waiver services and live in assisted living?
faqs.answer: Yes, assisted living is one of the covered services under the AD Waiver. Your loved one can use waiver funding to help pay for assisted living costs, but they cannot receive duplicate services — if assisted living provides personal care, the waiver won't pay separately for personal care services.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's care needs increase after they're on the waiver?
faqs.answer: Your service coordinator will reassess your loved one's needs and can adjust the Person-Centered Care Plan to include additional services. However, the total cost cannot exceed nursing facility rates, so some people may eventually need to transition to a nursing home if their needs become too expensive.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one who has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply on behalf of someone who cannot manage their own affairs. You may need to provide legal documentation such as power of attorney, guardianship papers, or become a designated representative through the application process.

faqs.question: How do we choose service providers once approved for the waiver?
faqs.answer: Your assigned service coordinator will provide a list of approved waiver providers in your area. You can choose which providers to work with based on your loved one's needs, preferences, and provider availability. Different regions may have different provider networks.

faqs.question: What if we disagree with the functional assessment decision?
faqs.answer: If your loved one is denied for not meeting nursing facility level of care, you have the right to appeal the decision. Contact (877) 667-6266 within 30 days to request an appeal hearing. You can also request a new assessment if your loved one's condition has changed significantly.

programs.name: Nebraska Community Aging Services Act
programs.tagline: Home care, meals, and transportation services to help your loved one stay independent at home.

localEntities.name: State Unit on Aging (oversees program)
Multiple Area Agencies on Aging throughout Nebraska (specific locations not listed in available sources)

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 60+ and struggling to manage daily tasks like cleaning, cooking, or getting to appointments, Nebraska's Community Aging Services Act provides homemaker services, meal delivery, transportation, and adult day care to help them stay in their home safely. Services include congregate activities at senior centers, home-delivered meals, and community-based support designed to promote independence.

Unlike Medicaid, this program has no published statewide income limits — eligibility is determined individually by your local Area Agency on Aging based on your loved one's needs and available community resources. Priority goes to those in greatest need, but specific services and availability vary significantly by region. You'll need an assessment and service plan before any services can begin.

faqs.question: Can my loved one receive services if they have too much income for Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Possibly. Unlike Medicaid, the Community Aging Services Act has no published statewide income limits. Your local Area Agency on Aging makes eligibility decisions based on individual circumstances, community resources, and local standards. Some services like congregate meals have no income requirements at all.

faqs.question: What if my loved one lives in a rural area with limited services?
faqs.answer: Service availability varies significantly by region and local Area Agency on Aging resources. Rural areas may have fewer options or longer wait times. Call your local Area Agency to learn what's actually available in your specific area — don't assume all services are offered everywhere.

faqs.question: How long does the assessment process take?
faqs.answer: Assessment timing isn't specified and varies by Area Agency on Aging workload and staffing. The assessment must be completed before any individual services can begin, and a service plan must be developed. Contact your local office for current wait times for assessments.

faqs.question: Can I apply for my loved one if they have dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can typically initiate the process on behalf of aging loved ones, especially those with cognitive impairment. The Area Agency on Aging will work with you to assess your loved one's needs and circumstances. You may need legal documentation if you're the official guardian or power of attorney.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's needs change after services start?
faqs.answer: Contact your Area Agency on Aging to request a reassessment and updated service plan. Needs can increase or decrease over time, and services should be adjusted accordingly. However, expanded services depend on available funding and resources in your area.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get both Community Aging Services and Medicaid services at the same time?
faqs.answer: This depends on the specific services and your loved one's Medicaid status. The Community Aging Services Act is separate from Medicaid, but there may be coordination or restrictions to avoid duplicate services. Discuss this with both your Area Agency on Aging and Medicaid case worker.

faqs.question: What if there's a waiting list for the services my loved one needs?
faqs.answer: Priority is given to those in greatest need, but services aren't guaranteed due to limited funding. Ask your Area Agency about current wait times and whether there are alternative services available while you wait. Some regions have immediate openings while others have extended waits.

faqs.question: Does my loved one have to accept all services in their plan, or can they choose specific ones?
faqs.answer: Your loved one typically can choose which services to accept from their developed service plan. However, some services may be bundled together or have prerequisites. Discuss preferences and concerns during the assessment process to create a plan that works for your family's situation.

programs.name: Aging & Disability Resource Center Act
programs.tagline: Free help finding and accessing long-term care services, benefits, and providers for your aging loved one.

programs.intro:
If your loved one needs long-term care in Nebraska, the Aging & Disability Resource Center (ADRC) provides free help navigating the system. Because Nebraska's long-term care programs are scattered across multiple agencies, ADRC serves as a single entry point to find services, understand options, and apply for benefits like Medicaid home care waivers.

ADRC counselors provide options counseling, help with benefit applications, and connect your loved one to local providers. They also assess whether your loved one qualifies for nursing facility level of care (required for most Medicaid waiver services). The service is free for anyone 60+ or with disabilities of any age, plus their family members and caregivers.

ADRC operates through regional Area Agencies on Aging across Nebraska. Each region has local counselors who know the providers and waitlists in your area.

faqs.question: Can I call ADRC on behalf of my loved one who has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family caregivers are explicitly eligible for ADRC services. The counselor can work with you to assess your loved one's needs and explore options, though some formal applications may require your loved one's signature or legal authorization.

faqs.question: Will ADRC help us apply for programs outside of Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes, ADRC provides assistance accessing all public benefits, not just Medicaid waivers. They can help with applications for SNAP, utility assistance, housing programs, and other services your loved one might qualify for.

faqs.question: What if my loved one doesn't qualify for nursing facility level of care?
faqs.answer: ADRC will still help you find other resources and services. They provide information on all long-term care options, including private-pay services, community programs, and support for family caregivers, even if your loved one doesn't qualify for Medicaid waivers.

programs.name: Aid to the Aged, Blind, and Disabled (AABD)
programs.tagline: Monthly cash payments and medical coverage for Nebraska seniors and disabled adults denied federal SSI

localEntities.name: Nebraska Department of Health and Human Services local offices

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 65+ or disabled and was denied federal SSI benefits because their disability is expected to last less than 12 months, Nebraska's AABD program provides monthly cash payments and medical coverage. This state program fills the gap for people who need financial help but don't qualify for federal assistance due to the short-term nature of their disability.

AABD also serves blind adults under 65 (visual acuity of 20/200 or less) and aged adults 65+ who meet income and asset requirements. The program requires a disability determination from Social Security or Nebraska's State Review Team, and your loved one must be ineligible for Medicaid through other pathways to qualify for the disability component.

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply for AABD if they're already getting some Social Security benefits?
faqs.answer: Yes, your loved one can receive Social Security retirement or disability benefits and still qualify for AABD if they meet the income and asset limits. The AABD payment amount will be calculated based on their other income sources and living arrangement.

faqs.question: What's the difference between being denied federal SSI and qualifying for AABD?
faqs.answer: Federal SSI requires disabilities to last 12+ months. AABD covers people whose disabilities are expected to last less than 12 months but still need financial assistance. If Social Security denied your loved one's SSI application due to disability duration, they may qualify for Nebraska's AABD instead.

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house and still get AABD?
faqs.answer: Yes, the home your loved one lives in typically doesn't count toward the asset limits. However, income and asset limits do apply, and the specific amounts aren't detailed in state materials — contact Nebraska DHHS at (402) 471-3121 for current limits.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's disability lasts longer than initially expected?
faqs.answer: If your loved one's condition worsens or lasts longer than 12 months, they should reapply for federal SSI benefits. They can continue receiving AABD while pursuing federal benefits, but will need to cooperate with obtaining all available third-party payments.

faqs.question: Can I apply for AABD on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can help with the application process. You can call Nebraska DHHS at (402) 471-3121 to discuss acting as a representative for your loved one, but you'll need proper authorization and documentation of your loved one's need for assistance.

faqs.question: Does living in assisted living affect AABD payments?
faqs.answer: Yes, your loved one's living arrangement affects the payment amount. Whether they live at home, in an apartment, or in assisted living will impact how much they receive, as payments are calculated based on the federal benefit rate and living situation.

programs.name: Social Service for Aged and Disabled Adults (SSAD)
programs.tagline: Free in-home care, adult day programs, and transportation for Nebraska seniors who don't qualify for Medicaid.

localEntities.name: DHHS Local Offices statewide

programs.intro:
If your loved one is aged, blind, or disabled and needs help staying independent but doesn't qualify for Medicaid or other assistance programs, Nebraska's SSAD program provides free services based on income and need. SSAD covers chore services (light housekeeping, shopping, meal prep, personal care), adult day care programs (minimum 3 hours daily), homemaker services, and transportation to medical appointments and community resources.

This is specifically designed to fill gaps for people who fall through the cracks of other programs. Because SSAD serves those ineligible for Medicaid waivers, it often helps people whose income is slightly too high for other assistance but still can't afford private care. Services are authorized based on individual assessment rather than fixed hours or dollar limits.

SSAD is administered through local DHHS offices statewide, with services coordinated locally based on your loved one's specific needs and demonstrated inability to perform these tasks independently.

faqs.question: How is SSAD different from the Medicaid AD Waiver program?
faqs.answer: SSAD specifically serves people who don't qualify for Medicaid waivers, often because their income is slightly too high. While AD Waiver is funded by Medicaid, SSAD is state-funded and designed to fill gaps for those who fall through the cracks of other programs.

faqs.question: What if my loved one was denied Medicaid but still needs help with daily tasks?
faqs.answer: This is exactly what SSAD is designed for. Bring your Medicaid denial letter when you apply — it helps demonstrate that you need SSAD services because other programs aren't available to you.

faqs.question: How many hours of service will my loved one receive?
faqs.answer: SSAD doesn't have fixed service hours. Instead, services are authorized based on your loved one's individual assessment and demonstrated need. The program tailors services to what your loved one specifically needs rather than offering preset packages.

faqs.question: Can a family member apply for SSAD on behalf of an elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can help with the application process. Contact your local DHHS office to discuss what documentation you'll need if you're applying on behalf of your loved one.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's needs change after they're approved for SSAD?
faqs.answer: Contact your local DHHS office to request a reassessment. Since services are based on individual need, they can be adjusted up or down as your loved one's situation changes.

faqs.question: Does my loved one have to choose between SSAD and other programs?
faqs.answer: SSAD is specifically for people who don't qualify for other assistance programs. However, they may be able to receive SSAD alongside some programs that aren't considered "assistance" — discuss your specific situation with DHHS.

faqs.question: What if the adult day care program requires more than 3 hours but my loved one can only handle less?
faqs.answer: The 3-hour minimum is a program requirement for adult day care services. If your loved one can't handle that duration, focus on the other SSAD services like chore services, homemaker services, or transportation assistance.

faqs.question: How long does it take to start receiving services after approval?
faqs.answer: Processing time isn't specified for SSAD, but related DHHS programs typically process within 90 days. Contact your local office for current timelines and ask about expedited processing if there are urgent needs.

stateOverview.intro: Nebraska offers 17 programs to help your aging loved one stay independent and reduce your family's caregiving costs. These range from substantial financial benefits like SNAP food assistance (up to $546/month for couples) and Medicare premium coverage, to free resources like legal help and Medicare counseling available to all seniors. Most benefit programs have income limits, while resources like Nebraska SHIP counseling and Legal Services for Seniors are free regardless of income. Start by checking the highest-impact programs first — Medicare Savings Programs and SNAP can provide immediate monthly savings, while home care waivers can prevent or delay nursing home placement.

stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Nebraska's programs fall into two categories: benefits that require qualification (like SNAP food assistance or Medicaid home care waivers based on income and medical need) and free resources available to all seniors regardless of income (like Nebraska SHIP Medicare counseling, Legal Services for Seniors, and Long-Term Care Ombudsman advocacy). Start with free resources to get expert guidance, then apply for qualified benefits your loved one may be eligible for.
